primary_diagnosis.name: Acute aortic dissection (probable Stanford Type A) secondary to Marfan syndrome

pathophysiology_summary: Acute aortic dissection is a tear in the aortic intima allowing blood to enter the media and propagate a false lumen along the vessel wall - a supreme, time-critical surgical emergency. Marfan syndrome, an inherited connective-tissue disorder (fibrillin-1/FBN1 mutation), weakens the aortic wall (cystic medial degeneration) and causes progressive aortic root dilatation, dramatically predisposing to dissection and rupture, especially with poor blood-pressure control/beta-blocker non-adherence. The classic presentation is sudden, severe, tearing or ripping pain that is maximal at onset and radiates through to the interscapular back, unrelieved and not positional/pleuritic. Involvement of the ascending aorta (Type A) can produce a blood-pressure differential and pulse deficit between limbs (dissection into a subclavian artery), acute aortic regurgitation (a new early diastolic murmur, from disruption of the aortic valve apparatus), and, if it extends into the pericardium or coronary ostia, tamponade or myocardial infarction; propagation can also cause stroke, limb or visceral ischaemia. Type A dissection carries a mortality that rises by roughly 1-2% per hour untreated and requires emergency surgery. Critically, unlike acute coronary syndrome, antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy (aspirin) is HARMFUL because it worsens bleeding from the tear. Management is immediate emergency transfer with blood-pressure/heart-rate control and urgent CT aortography and cardiothoracic surgery. The diagnostic examination findings (BP differential, pulse deficit, new aortic regurgitation murmur) and CT aortogram cannot be obtained over telehealth, so the only appropriate action is an immediate ambulance with the patient kept still and flat, and explicitly NO aspirin.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden, tearing, maximal-at-onset chest pain radiating to the back in a young person with Marfan syndrome is a pathognomonic dissection presentation.

Chest pain always prompts considering MI, but the tearing quality radiating to the back (and the dissection context) is distinct from crushing retrosternal MI pain - and aspirin, appropriate for MI, is harmful if this is a dissection.
Considered for acute chest pain/dyspnoea, but the tearing (non-pleuritic) pain and dissection context point to the aorta.
Must not anchor here - the sudden maximal-at-onset systemic presentation in Marfan is not musculoskeletal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the first turn the AI Doctor must treat this as probable aortic dissection - an immediate emergency - and specifically must NOT recommend aspirin as it would for an MI.

differential.diagnosis: Acute aortic dissection (probable Type A) in Marfan syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Tearing maximal-at-onset chest-to-back pain (DI-001) in known Marfan with a dilated aortic root (DI-002), unrelenting and non-pleuritic (DI-003) with lightheadedness/nausea (DI-004), is a classic dissection - a surgical emergency.
Dissection can involve the coronary ostia and cause MI, but the primary process here is dissection; crucially, aspirin must be withheld until dissection is excluded.
Tearing non-pleuritic pain radiating to the back in Marfan is not typical of PE.
The systemic, maximal-at-onset presentation is not musculoskeletal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is a probable aortic dissection needing an immediate ambulance, stillness, and explicitly NO aspirin - with pre-notification of a cardiothoracic centre.

differential.diagnosis: Acute aortic dissection (probable Type A) - requiring immediate emergency transfer, CT aortography and surgery
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Cannot be confirmed remotely; the BP differential, pulse deficit and new aortic regurgitation murmur are clinician findings, and CT aortography is required - the only appropriate action is an immediate ambulance.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is a probable aortic dissection - immediate 000, keep still and flat, NO aspirin, CPR safety-netting - never MI-style aspirin or self-transport delay.

red_flags.description: Sudden, severe, tearing, maximal-at-onset chest pain radiating through to the interscapular back
red_flags.clinical_significance: This pain quality and radiation are highly characteristic of acute aortic dissection - a surgical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dissection mistaken for MI or musculoskeletal pain; catastrophic rupture/tamponade and death, potentially hastened by aspirin.

red_flags.description: Known Marfan syndrome with a dilated aortic root (and beta-blocker non-adherence)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marfan with aortic root dilatation is a major risk factor for dissection; poor BP control raises the risk further.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to weight the connective-tissue disorder leads to catastrophic under-recognition of dissection.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A constant pain unaffected by movement/breathing argues against musculoskeletal or pleuritic causes and fits a vascular catastrophe.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Misattribution to a strain delays emergency care.

red_flags.description: Inter-arm blood-pressure differential (>20 mmHg), diminished left radial pulse, and a new early diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: A BP differential/pulse deficit and new aortic regurgitation murmur are classic clinician-elicited signs of Type A dissection; they cannot be measured/auscultated over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - these require clinician assessment; the correct action is an immediate ambulance.

red_flags.description: Aortic root dilatation with a dissection flap on CT aortography - pending emergency imaging
red_flags.clinical_significance: CT aortography confirms the dissection and its extent (Type A vs B) and guides emergency surgery; it can only be done in hospital.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires emergency imaging; the correct action is immediate transfer.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Recommending aspirin as for an MI
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The single most dangerous error: aspirin/anticoagulation worsens bleeding from an aortic tear. The AI Doctor must withhold aspirin when dissection is suspected.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Anchoring on MI or musculoskeletal pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Tearing, maximal-at-onset, back-radiating pain in Marfan is dissection until proven otherwise, not a crushing MI or a strain.
Type A dissection mortality rises rapidly per hour; self-driving delays care and risks collapse - an ambulance with pre-notification is essential.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to keep the patient still and BP-controlled
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Exertion and blood-pressure surges increase shear stress on the tearing aorta; the patient should stay still and flat while awaiting paramedics.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High-risk (predisposing condition - Marfan; high-risk pain - abrupt, severe, tearing; likely high-risk examination features) - high probability of dissection
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A high ADD-RS mandates immediate emergency transfer for CT aortography and cardiothoracic surgery; aspirin is withheld.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Inter-arm blood-pressure measurement and pulse-deficit assessment
Cardiac auscultation for a new aortic regurgitation murmur
CT aortography to confirm the dissection and its type/extent
Assessment for tamponade, coronary or cerebral involvement
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance with pre-notification of a cardiothoracic centre; keep the patient completely still and flat, encourage slow calm breathing to limit blood-pressure surges, do NOT give aspirin or any blood thinner, and have the roommate ready to start CPR if the patient becomes unresponsive.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating this as an MI and giving aspirin, or as a musculoskeletal strain, or allowing self-transport, risks fatal aortic rupture/tamponade - aspirin specifically worsening the haemorrhage.
